content: Mr. Market is your servant not your master. The market offers to buy or sell shares daily at different prices. You are free to accept or ignore. Most of the time doing nothing is wisest. Treat price fluctuations as opportunities not threats.

Margin of safety: never buy a security unless price is 25-30% below conservatively calculated intrinsic value. This buffer protects against analytical errors, bad luck, and market volatility. The central concept of investing.

Short run market is a voting machine (popularity), long run it is a weighing machine (fundamental value). Focus on weighing. Do not let short-term price action influence long-term investment decisions.
Defensive investor: maintain 25-75% stocks vs bonds split. Never below 25% stocks (inflation risk) or above 75% (crash risk). Rebalance annually to targets. Simple, disciplined, effective.
Defensive stock selection criteria: large company, strong finances, 10+ yr dividend record, no earnings deficit in 10 yrs, 33% earnings growth over 10 yrs, P/E below 15, P/E x P/B below 22.5.
Enterprising investor: buy stocks trading below net working capital (current assets minus total liabilities). These cigar-butt offers near-zero downside with meaningful upside.
The investor chief problem and worst enemy is likely himself. Emotional discipline trumps analytical ability. Develop sound intellectual framework and keep emotions from corroding it.
Never invest in a company without understanding its business model. If you cannot explain what the company does in one sentence, do not buy it regardless of price.
Dollar-cost averaging: invest equal amounts at regular intervals regardless of market level. Eliminates market timing and reduces average cost basis over full market cycles.
Diversify holdings: minimum 10 stocks maximum 30. Below 10 too concentrated. Above 30 impossible to monitor each company deeply. Sweet spot is 15-20 for enterprising investors.

If you own stocks you will see values drop 30-50% multiple times in your investing lifetime. This is normal. Prepare psychologically and financially. Declines are opportunities not existential threats.

Speculation vs investing: an operation is speculation unless it ensures safety of principal AND adequate return. Know which you are doing. Most market participants speculate while thinking they invest.
Earnings stability: look for companies with positive earnings every year for 10+ consecutive years. Any deficit in the earnings record over a decade is a disqualifying red flag.
Graham number: sqrt(22.5 x EPS x BVPS). Maximum price to pay for a stock. If current price exceeds Graham number, stock is overvalued. Quick quantitative filter.
Debt check: total long-term debt should be less than net current assets. High leverage amplifies losses asymmetrically. Avoid overleveraged companies especially in uncertain environments.

Enterprising tactics: buy in depressed markets sell in enthusiastic ones; buy growth at reasonable prices; exploit special situations like spin-offs mergers restructurings. Requires more work but higher returns.
Never borrow to buy stocks on margin. Leverage converts safe investment into dangerous speculation. The intelligent investor never needs margin to achieve satisfactory returns.
The defensive investor must expect to be wrong frequently. Key is framework producing satisfactory average results long-term, not being right on every individual decision.
Dividend preference: prefer companies paying consistent growing dividends. Dividend yield should be meaningful. Dividends provide the only guaranteed return component from stock ownership.
When euphoric increase bonds. When depressed increase stocks. The crowd is usually wrong at extremes. Use sentiment as a contrarian allocation signal, not a timing tool.
Net-net investing: if current assets minus ALL liabilities exceeds market cap, you are buying the business for less than liquidation value. Extreme margin of safety with high probability of profit.
Inflation hedge: stocks generally protect better than bonds over long periods. But do not overpay based on inflation fears. Margin of safety still required regardless of inflation outlook.
Convert bond allocation based on market conditions: when stocks are clearly cheap favor stocks. When stocks are clearly expensive favor bonds. When uncertain maintain 50-50.
Do not let Mr. Market dictate your actions. The market exists to serve you not instruct you. If you cannot resist checking prices daily, set a rule to check monthly at most.

Six stock categories: (1) Slow growers 2-4% (2) Stalwarts 10-12% (3) Fast growers 20-25% small aggressive (4) Cyclicals tied to business cycle (5) Turnarounds near-bankruptcy recovery (6) Asset plays hidden valuable assets. Each requires different strategy.
PEG ratio: P/E divided by expected earnings growth rate. PEG below 1.0 undervalued, 1.0 fair, above 1.0 overvalued. Single best valuation metric for growth stocks. Never overpay for growth.

Invest in what you know. Average investors find great stocks years before professionals by observing everyday life: restaurants, stores, products used. Your edge is personal experience and ground-level observation.
Fast growers: look for 20-25% annual growth with long runway. Best fast growers are in boring non-sexy industries with less competition, less analyst coverage, more mispricing potential.
Cyclical investing: buy when economy in recession and earnings depressed. Sell at peak earnings when everyone optimistic. Timing is everything with cyclicals. Never hold cyclicals long-term.
Turnaround plays: companies emerged from bankruptcy or with viable core business obscured by bad segment. Stock can go from 6 to 0 or 6 to 60. Asymmetric payoff requires careful sizing.
Asset plays: companies whose real estate, subsidiaries, cash, or brand names worth more than stock price. Balance sheet hides value market ignores. Requires deep research to uncover.
Stalwarts: large companies growing 10-12%. Buy 30-40% off highs. They recover from most dips. Dependable not exciting. Sell when growth slows below 6% or P/E gets extreme.
Two-minute drill: explain in 2 minutes why you own each stock. If story still makes sense hold. If story has deteriorated sell. Never own what you cannot explain simply.
Red flags: next big thing stocks, diworsification acquisitions, single-customer dependency, hot IPOs in trendy industries, whisper stocks with no earnings. These destroy capital consistently.
Buy what you know but do homework. Visit stores, talk to customers, read 10-K. Personal experience is starting point not finish line. Back intuition with hard research.
Tenbagger criteria: small company growing fast, plenty of room to expand, non-sexy industry, institutional ownership below 30%, insiders buying, simple repeatable business model.
Key financials: debt-to-equity below 33%, strong cash position, P/E at or below growth rate, consistent earnings growth 5+ years. Cash-rich low-debt companies have optionality.
Portfolio construction: own 3-10 stocks across categories. More than 10 hard to monitor. Fewer than 3 too concentrated. Match stock category to portfolio role and your risk tolerance.
Best buy opportunities: market dropped 10%+, good company reports clearly temporary bad quarter, sector rotation causes unrelated sell-off. Buy quality dips not garbage dips.
Ignore interest rate predictions, economic forecasts, market direction calls. Nobody predicts these consistently. Focus on finding great individual companies regardless of macro noise.

Sell rules by category: stalwarts when growth slows or P/E extreme, fast growers when growth below 15% or too big, cyclicals at peak earnings, turnarounds when turnaround complete, asset plays when asset monetized.
Silliest things said about stocks: it already dropped so it cannot go lower, it was 50 so it will go back, always darkest before dawn. These are dangerous rationalizations that destroy capital.
Quarterly story check: are earnings growing as expected? Is expansion on track? Debt manageable? Insiders buying or selling? If story intact ignore the stock price completely.
Turnaround debt check: turnaround company paying down debt is getting healthier. One taking on more debt is getting sicker. Watch the balance sheet before the income statement.
Average investors have advantages over Wall Street: they can invest in small caps institutions ignore, they have personal experience with products, they have no career risk from unconventional picks.
Circle of competence: only invest in businesses you understand. Know boundaries of what you know and do not. Stay within your circle. Size of circle does not matter, knowing its boundaries does.
Business tenet 1: is the business simple and understandable? You should understand how the company makes money, its competitive position, and industry dynamics in plain terms.
Business tenet 2: consistent operating history. Look for companies doing the same thing successfully for many years. Avoid dramatic turnarounds or frequent strategy changes.
Business tenet 3: favorable long-term prospects. Key is economic moats - durable competitive advantages: brand identity, patent protection, cost advantages, switching costs, network effects.
Four moat types: (1) intangible assets brands patents licenses (2) switching costs (3) network effects (4) cost advantages from process location scale. Evaluate moat durability over 10+ years.

Management tenet: rational capital allocation. If management cannot earn above-average returns on retained earnings they should pay dividends or buy back shares. Test: each dollar retained must create at least one dollar market value.
Management honesty: admit mistakes openly. Communicate clearly. Avoid accounting gimmicks. If management blames poor results on external factors be very cautious about their competence.
Institutional imperative: the tendency to mindlessly imitate peer behavior. Great managers think independently. Do not follow the herd in acquisitions compensation or strategy decisions.
Owner earnings = net income + depreciation - capex. True cash flow available to owners. Ignore reported EPS without adjusting for maintenance capex. This is what the business actually earns.
Look for consistently high ROE with low debt. High ROE with little leverage indicates genuine competitive advantage. High ROE with high leverage is a red flag masquerading as quality.
Calculate intrinsic value using discounted cash flow. Use 10-year Treasury rate as discount rate. Higher rate = lower value. DCF is gold standard for business valuation.
Buy at significant discount to intrinsic value. Margin of safety is non-negotiable. Wonderful company at fair price beats fair company at wonderful price. Quality + Value = Optimal.
Favorite holding period: forever. If not willing to own 10 years do not own 10 minutes. Short-term trading is a loser game after transaction costs and taxes.
Be fearful when others greedy and greedy when others fearful. Maximum pessimism = best buying. Maximum optimism = best selling. Requires extreme emotional discipline.
Portfolio concentration: put most money in best 5-6 ideas. Diversification is protection against ignorance. If you know what you are doing concentration produces superior returns.
Think of stocks as businesses not ticker symbols. Buying fractional ownership in real business with real assets and earnings. This mindset eliminates most emotional trading mistakes.
Rule 1: Never lose money. Rule 2: Never forget Rule 1. Preserving capital is paramount. A 50% loss requires 100% gain to recover. Asymmetric math demands capital preservation first.
Never use leverage. Borrowed money is how smart people go broke. Even at 99-to-1 odds the 1% event wipes you out if leveraged. Patience replaces leverage as the accelerator.
Owner earnings valuation: what would you pay for a private business earning X per year with Y growth rate? Apply same logic to public stocks. Private market value anchors your analysis.
Goodwill: economic goodwill (brand reputation customer loyalty) far exceeds accounting goodwill. Coca-Cola economic goodwill dwarfs book value. This is what creates compounding value.
Sell only when: (1) analytical error made (2) business fundamentals permanently deteriorated (3) found significantly better opportunity. Never sell based on price alone or market predictions.
Arbitrage: announced M&A deals with calculable spread and low deal-failure risk. Systematic source of returns. Works when market is uncertain about deal completion.
Avoid overpaying for acquisitions. Most acquisitions destroy value through competitive bidding. Best deals negotiated privately at fair prices with aligned incentives.
Book value vs intrinsic value: book value is backward-looking accounting. Intrinsic value is forward-looking economics. They often diverge greatly. Focus on intrinsic value always.
Avoid technology unless within circle of competence. Sitting out entire sectors outside your understanding is perfectly fine. Missing a boom beats losing capital in a bust.
Look-through earnings: your share of undistributed earnings of investee companies count as real earnings. Reported dividends understate true economic earnings of quality holdings.
Junk bonds and leverage: avoid high-yield bonds issued at par to fund acquisitions. The extra yield does not compensate for default risk. Buffett calls them financial weapons of mass destruction.
Double-barreled approach: prefer 100% acquisition of great businesses at fair prices, but also buy partial ownership (public stocks) when available below intrinsic value on the open market.
The stock market is a device for transferring money from the impatient to the patient. Time in the market beats timing the market when holding quality businesses.
Tax-efficient holding: long-term capital gains taxed at lower rates. Frequent trading creates tax drag that compounds against you over time. Hold quality positions for years not weeks.
Point 1: Products or services with sufficient market potential for sizable sales increase for several years. Growth requires large addressable market. Without TAM growth is capped.
Point 2: Management determination to develop new products when current lines growth potential exploited. Continuous innovation pipeline is essential for sustained above-average growth.
Point 3: R&D effectiveness relative to size. R&D spending as % of revenue compared to peers. Quality of research matters more than quantity. Patent output and product pipeline are key metrics.
Point 5: Above-average profit margins relative to industry. Margins should be stable or expanding. A company with 15% margins in a 10% industry has a structural competitive edge.
Point 6: Active margin improvement through cost control, pricing power, or operational efficiency. Management must be working to expand margins not just maintain them.
Point 8: Outstanding executive relations. Labor and executive team harmony is critical. A company at war with its employees will eventually fail to execute strategy.
Point 11: Industry-specific competitive advantages. Look for clues specific to the industry that indicate how outstanding the company may be relative to competition. Moats vary by sector.
Point 12: Management talks straight to shareholders. Candor about both good and bad news is essential. Management hiding bad news will eventually lose credibility and stock value.
Point 14: Management integrity. Candid about mistakes as well as successes. Integrity is the most important management quality. Without it all other qualities become worthless or dangerous.
Scuttlebutt research: talk to competitors, suppliers, customers, former employees, industry experts to build comprehensive company picture before investing. Go far beyond annual reports and filings.
Concentrate in best ideas: own 10-12 stocks maximum. Each should represent highest conviction. Over-diversification dilutes returns and prevents deep monitoring of holdings.
Buy and hold for decades. Do not sell for market declines, short-term earnings misses, or recessions. Sell only when: analysis was wrong, management deteriorated, or business fundamentally changed.
Growth at reasonable price: even the best company is not a good investment at any price. Quality must justify price. Fisher would pay up for quality but not unlimited amounts.
Point 7: Outstanding labor and personnel relations. Happy productive employees are a significant competitive advantage. High turnover is a leading indicator of future problems.
Point 9: Management depth. A one-man show is dangerous. Look for depth of talent and clear succession plan. Multiple capable leaders reduce key-person risk.
Point 10: Cost analysis and financial controls. Cannot manage what cannot measure. Excellent cost accounting leads to better decisions and sustainable margins.
Point 13: Beware of dilution. Will growth require significant equity financing that cancels shareholder benefit? Excessive stock issuance destroys per-share value even in growing companies.
Do not over-diversify beyond 10-12 stocks. Beyond that additional diversification reduces returns without meaningfully reducing risk. Focus capital on highest conviction ideas.
Patience is the key to investment success. Biggest gains come from holding great companies through multiple cycles. Do not be shaken out by temporary setbacks or market noise.
Best investments: management with clear competitive advantage reinvesting profits to widen it. Reinvested earnings should generate above-average returns on incremental capital.
5-Step Process: (1) Set clear goals (2) Identify problems preventing goals (3) Diagnose root causes (4) Design solutions (5) Execute. Iterative process applies to trading investing and all of life.
Embrace reality and deal with it. Do not let wishes or fears distort perception. Pain + Reflection = Progress. Every mistake reveals a principle you are missing. Study failures systematically.
Be radically open-minded. Biggest threat is your own blind spots. Actively seek best opposing arguments. If you cannot articulate the other side better than they can, you are not thinking clearly.
Believability-weighted decisions: weight opinions by track record. Someone who succeeded 3+ times is more believable than a theorist. Give more weight to credible voices in all decisions.
Radical transparency: put all reasoning on the table. Best decisions come from considering all perspectives without ego. In trading: document every trade rationale and review honestly.
Systematic decision-making: convert principles into algorithms and rules. Test historically. Follow mechanically. This removes emotion and ensures consistency across all market conditions.
Everything cycles. Economies cycle between expansion and contraction. Markets cycle between greed and fear. Credit cycles drive most movements. Know where you are in the cycle to position correctly.
Holy grail of investing: 15-20 uncorrelated return streams. Reduces risk 80% without reducing expected return. Find assets and strategies that zig when others zag. Correlation is the enemy.
All-Weather concept: 30% stocks 40% long bonds 15% intermediate bonds 7.5% gold 7.5% commodities. Balance performs in all economic environments: growth/inflation up or down.

Risk parity: allocate by risk not capital. Each asset class contributes equally to portfolio risk. Bonds less volatile so hold more to equalize risk. True diversification requires equal risk contribution.
Pain signals something wrong. Do not avoid it. Study it. If you feel pain in a trade analyze why. The pain often reveals a principle you need to learn before proceeding.
Thoughtful disagreement: willingness to have ideas challenged is the single most valuable decision-making trait. Seek smartest people who disagree and understand their reasoning deeply.
Economic machine: (1) productivity growth trend (2) long-term debt cycle 50-75 yrs (3) short-term debt cycle 5-8 yrs. Understanding position in each cycle guides optimal asset allocation.
Never bet the farm. Even at 99% certainty size positions so being wrong does not knock you out. Survival is prerequisite for long-term compounding of returns.
Two yous: the higher-level rational you must design systems the lower-level emotional you follows. Set rules and guardrails BEFORE emotional moments arrive. Pre-commit to process.
Backtest principles against history. Every investment decision should be tested against how similar situations played out in the past. If it did not work before reconsider it now.
Stress test portfolio: what if rates spike? Inflation runs hot? Pandemic? Correlations break down? Prepare for scenarios you have not personally experienced. Black swans are inevitable.

Nature is a machine. Economy and markets are machines with cause-effect relationships. Study these mechanisms rather than making emotional predictions. Understand the machine and outcomes become predictable.
Idea meritocracy: best ideas win regardless of who proposes them. Keep journal of ideas score on outcomes and let data reveal which approaches work. Meritocracy of evidence over opinion.
Leverage transforms manageable risk into existential risk. Survive 1982 by not being over-leveraged. Always maintain enough liquidity to survive being wrong for extended periods. Cash is optionality.
When central banks print money (QE) asset prices rise and correlations increase. When they tighten the reverse. Central bank policy is the dominant force in modern markets. Track it obsessively.
Successful people change in ways that allow continued achievement. Failed people think they already know the best way. Evolution requires humility and constant adaptation to new information.
Diversify across asset classes, geographies, time horizons, and strategies. No single bet should be able to materially impair total portfolio value. Position sizing is the ultimate risk tool.
Principles must be written down and explicit. If you cannot write your trading rules clearly you do not understand them well enough to execute consistently under stress.
Absolute momentum (time-series): asset has positive momentum if current price above price N months ago (typically 12). Only go long assets in uptrends. Shift to cash/bonds in downtrends.
Relative momentum (cross-sectional): rank assets by trailing returns and buy strongest performers. Winners tend to keep winning over 3-12 month horizons. Exploit continuation of trends.
Dual momentum combines both: only buy assets with positive absolute momentum AND strongest relative momentum. Filters false signals from each approach alone. Best of both worlds.
Risk-managed momentum: use absolute momentum as trend filter. When risk assets in downtrend shift to safe-haven assets. Cuts maximum drawdowns dramatically while capturing most upside.
Dual momentum timing: compare equities vs bonds over 12 months. If equities outperform hold equities. If bonds outperform or equities show negative absolute return shift to bonds entirely.

Lookback period: 12 months optimal based on extensive academic research. Shorter periods (1-3 months) too noisy. Longer periods (24+ months) miss regime changes. 12-month sweet spot confirmed across markets.

Momentum crashes: momentum strategies vulnerable to sudden reversals during market recoveries. Highest risk at bottom when losers suddenly become winners. Absolute momentum filter helps avoid this trap.
Global equity momentum: compare US, international developed, and emerging markets. Allocate to region(s) with strongest dual momentum. Captures global rotation and avoids weak regions.
Rebalance monthly or when momentum signals change. Frequent rebalancing captures turning points faster. Small cost of rebalancing is far outweighed by benefit of catching regime changes early.
Dual momentum shows Sharpe ratio 0.5-0.8 higher than buy-and-hold with max drawdowns reduced 40-60%. Robust across decades of data. One of the most well-documented investment strategies.
Smart beta caution: factor premiums (value size quality) are time-varying and can underperform for extended periods. Momentum is robust but still requires risk management overlay.
Asset selection: use broad liquid ETFs for major asset classes. Individual stocks have too much idiosyncratic risk. ETF momentum captures systematic returns more reliably and cheaply.
Risk parity with momentum: allocate equal risk per asset class then use momentum to time allocation. Combines stability of risk parity with return enhancement of momentum signals.
Biggest challenge is following system during drawdowns and underperformance. Dual momentum underperforms buy-and-hold during strong bull markets. Accept this as cost of avoiding bear markets.
Momentum strongest in 3-12 month range. Very short-term (days) is noise. Very long-term (3-5 years) tends to reverse. The sweet spot for trend following is around 12 months consistently.
Drawdown management: dual momentum typically limits max drawdown to 15-25% vs 40-55% for buy-and-hold. Psychologically easier to stay invested through crises when drawdowns are contained.
Robustness: dual momentum works across stocks bonds commodities REITs, across US international emerging markets, and across different time periods. Real edge not data mining.
Works because exploits behavioral biases: anchoring, herding, disposition effect. These biases are hardwired and persistent which is why momentum has worked for over 200 years of market data.
Transaction costs: use low-cost ETFs and minimize turnover. Rebalance no more than monthly. Total strategy costs should be under 0.5% annually including fund expenses and trading.
Momentum works best in trending markets struggles in choppy range-bound. When both absolute and relative signals are mixed reduce position sizes or hold more in cash/bonds as defensive posture.
C = Current quarterly earnings: look for 25%+ increase in current quarter EPS vs same quarter prior year. The bigger the acceleration the better. This is the single most important earnings signal.
A = Annual earnings growth: look for 25%+ annual EPS growth over past 3-5 years. ROE should be 17%+ annually. Consistency matters. Avoid companies with erratic or declining annual earnings.
N = New: newer companies, new products, new management, new price highs. Innovation drives stock performance. Buy stocks making new highs off properly formed bases not new lows.
S = Supply and demand: daily trading volume should increase significantly on up days and decrease on down days. Big volume demand at key price points signals institutional accumulation.
L = Leader or laggard: use Relative Strength (RS) rating to identify leaders. Buy stocks with RS 80 or above (top 20% of performers). Avoid laggards with low RS. Industry group strength matters too.

I = Institutional sponsorship: look for stocks with increasing institutional ownership by top-performing funds. At least 3-5 institutional sponsors needed. Avoid stocks with declining institutional interest.

M = Market direction: determine the general market trend before making any purchases. 3 out of 4 stocks follow the market direction. Do not fight the trend. Buy in confirmed uptrends sell in downtrends.
Cup with handle pattern: stock forms U-shaped cup (7-65 weeks) then a slight downward drift (handle). Buy on breakout above handle with volume 50%+ above average. Most reliable chart pattern.

Buy point: purchase at pivot point (10 cents above handle high) on breakout day with heavy volume. Volume should be at least 50% above average daily volume. If volume is weak the breakout will likely fail.
Cut every loss at 7-8% without exception. This is the most important rule. No hoping, no praying, no averaging down. The 7-8% stop protects you from catastrophic losses that destroy portfolios.
Sell rules for profits: sell when stock rises 20-25% from proper buy point. In a bull market let winners run longer with trailing stops. Take at least partial profits at 20% gain.
Never average down on a losing position. If a stock drops 7-8% from your buy point sell immediately. Averaging down doubles your risk on a position that has already proven you wrong.
Money management: do not diversify too much. Concentrate in 5-6 leading stocks. More than 10 positions makes it impossible to generate market-beating returns. Focus on quality not quantity.
Handle should form in upper half of cup with volume drying up. Low volume in handle indicates selling pressure has been absorbed. The tighter the handle the better. Ideal handle decline is 10-15%.
Avoid buying stocks that have formed late-stage bases (3rd or 4th). First and second bases are most reliable. Later bases indicate the stock is extended and more likely to fail.
Industry group selection: buy leading stocks in leading industry groups. When a group is hot 2-3 stocks in that group will outperform. Check if your stock industry group rank is in top 20%.

Follow the market averages daily. When market is in confirmed uptrend build positions. When distribution days accumulate (market down on above-average volume) get defensive. Market direction trumps all.
Sell when stock breaks below 50-day moving average on heavy volume after a big run. This signals institutional selling. Do not wait for a 7-8% loss if the technical action tells you to exit.
Do not buy cheap low-priced stocks. Quality stocks trade at higher prices for a reason. Low-priced stocks are usually low-priced because the companies are poor quality with bad fundamentals.
Watch for earnings surprises: stocks that beat earnings estimates by 5%+ and gap up on heavy volume are strong candidates. Post-earnings gap ups with volume are among the most powerful buy signals.
Double-bottom pattern: second reliable buy pattern after cup-with-handle. Stock forms W-shape. Buy at pivot 10 cents above the middle peak. Volume must confirm the breakout.
Study the greatest stock market winners of all time. Learn their chart patterns, fundamentals, and what made them successful. Model proven winners rather than trying to discover new methods.
Never use margin until you have 3+ years of profitable trading experience. Margin amplifies losses just as it amplifies gains. Most beginners who use margin get wiped out.
Do not listen to stock tips, TV pundits, or analyst upgrades. Do your own research using charts and fundamentals. By the time news reaches you the smart money has already acted.

Investment returns come from two sources: dividends and earnings growth. Speculative returns (P/E changes) are a net zero over time. Own businesses for their earnings power not for price appreciation.
Costs matter enormously. Every dollar paid in fees is a dollar less in returns. Over 30 years a 1% fee difference can reduce terminal wealth by 25-30%. Minimize all investment costs relentlessly.
Index funds guarantee your fair share of market returns. Active management is a zero-sum game before costs and a negative-sum game after costs. Most active managers underperform their index.
Reversion to the mean: today star performers are tomorrow laggards. Hot funds cool off. Hot sectors revert. The market is brutally efficient at eliminating excess returns over time.
Simplicity is the master strategy. Own the entire market through a single total market index fund. This eliminates stock selection risk, manager risk, and style drift while capturing market returns.

Do not chase performance. Buying last year top-performing fund or sector is a reliable way to underperform. Past performance does not predict future results. This is the most violated rule in investing.
Compounding is the eighth wonder. Small differences in returns compound into enormous differences in wealth over decades. A 7% return grows to 10x in 33 years while 5% takes 45 years.
Taxes are costs too. High-turnover funds generate capital gains distributions that reduce after-tax returns. Low-turnover index funds are inherently more tax-efficient.
The stock market is not the economy. Short-term prices reflect sentiment and liquidity. Long-term prices reflect corporate earnings and dividends. Focus on the long-term relationship.
Asset allocation is the most important investment decision. The split between stocks and bonds determines 90%+ of portfolio return variation. Get this right and everything else is secondary.
Beware the tyranny of compounding costs. A 2% annual fee over 50 years consumes 63% of your gross returns. The financial industry extracts enormous wealth through fees that appear small.
Do not just do something, stand there. The best portfolio strategy is to buy and hold a diversified low-cost index fund. Activity is the enemy of investment returns.
ETFs can be dangerous for active traders. The ability to trade ETFs intraday tempts investors to speculate. Index investing works only if you hold and do not trade. Turnover is the enemy.
Bonds: keep bond allocation equal to your age. Bonds provide stability and income. Total bond market index fund is sufficient. Do not chase yield in high-risk bonds.

Stock market returns are anchored to corporate earnings growth of 5-6% real per year. Any return above that from speculation will eventually be given back. Invest for business returns not market returns.

Magic Formula: rank stocks by (1) Return on Capital (quality) and (2) Earnings Yield (cheapness). Buy 20-30 top-ranked stocks. Hold 1 year. Rebalance. Simple mechanical and effective over long periods.
Return on Capital = EBIT / (Net Working Capital + Net Fixed Assets). Measures how efficiently a business generates profits from its capital. Higher is better. Look for 25%+ ROC.
Earnings Yield = EBIT / Enterprise Value. Measures how cheap a stock is relative to its earnings power. Higher yield means cheaper. This is better than P/E because it accounts for debt and cash.
Good companies at bargain prices: the magic formula combines quality (ROC) with cheapness (EY). Buying cheap bad companies or expensive good companies both underperform. You need both.
Diversify across 20-30 magic formula stocks. Any individual stock can fail. The formula works statistically across a portfolio of cheap quality companies not on any single pick.

Magic Formula backtested 17 years (1988-2004): returned 30.8% annualized vs 12.4% for S&P 500. Top 30 stocks rebalanced annually. Works but requires patience through multi-year underperformance stretches.
The formula will underperform for 1-3 year periods. Most people cannot tolerate this and quit before the long-term edge materializes. Patience is the most important ingredient in formula investing.
Exclude utilities, financials, and ADRs from formula. These have distorted capital structures that make ROC and EY calculations misleading. Stick to operating businesses with clean financials.
The stock market is a place where money transfers from the impatient to the patient. If you can hold for 3-5 years the magic formula gives you a statistical edge over the market.
Why it works: the market overreacts to bad news (making good companies cheap) and over-extrapolates good news (making mediocre companies expensive). The formula systematically exploits both biases.
Use a minimum market cap filter ($50M+ for safety $200M+ for practical). Micro-caps have wide bid-ask spreads and liquidity issues that erode theoretical returns in practice.
Implementation: buy 5-7 stocks every 3 months over 9-12 months until you hold 20-30. This staggers entry and reduces timing risk. Sell after holding 1 year and replace with new formula picks.
Earnings yield comparison: a stock with 10% earnings yield is like buying a bond paying 10% with potential for growth. Compare earnings yield to bond yields for relative value assessment.
Do not cherry-pick from the list. Human nature makes us avoid the cheapest stocks (they look ugly). Buy all 20-30 names mechanically. The ugly ones often perform best.
ROC above 25% indicates a truly wonderful business. Earnings yield above 8-10% indicates a bargain price. Stocks scoring well on both are the best risk-adjusted investments in the market.
Rule 1: Do your homework. Never buy a stock without understanding the business model, competitive landscape, financial health, and valuation. Lazy research leads to painful losses.
Economic moats: sustainable competitive advantages that protect returns on capital. Wide moat = 10+ years of protected profitability. Narrow moat = 5-10 years. No moat = commodity business to avoid.
Five moat sources: (1) intangible assets (2) switching costs (3) network effects (4) cost advantages (5) efficient scale. Each creates barriers that prevent competitors from eroding profits.
Rule 2: Find economic moat. Companies without moats are commodities competing on price alone. Only moat companies can sustain above-average returns on capital over long periods.
Rule 3: Have a margin of safety. Even the best company is a bad investment at the wrong price. Valuation matters. Buy when price is below your estimate of fair value by at least 20-30%.

Four pillars of moat: (1) market share and cost position (2) intangible assets (3) switching costs (4) network effects. Analyze each for every potential investment. Durable moats command premium valuations.
Financial health checklist: debt-to-equity below industry average, interest coverage above 5x, current ratio above 1.5, consistent free cash flow generation, no excessive share dilution.
Valuation: estimate intrinsic value using discounted cash flow. Key inputs: revenue growth rate, operating margin, capex needs, discount rate. Run scenarios with conservative assumptions.
Management quality: evaluate capital allocation track record, insider ownership, compensation alignment with shareholders, and strategic decision-making history. Actions speak louder than words.
Rule 4: Hold for the long term. Transaction costs and taxes erode returns. Only sell when: moat has deteriorated, valuation is extremely stretched, or you found a significantly better opportunity.
Rule 5: Know when to sell. Sell when: (1) you made analytical error (2) fundamentals deteriorated (3) stock is massively overvalued (4) better opportunity exists. Never sell on price drops alone.

Sector analysis: understand industry structure before analyzing individual companies. Attractive industries have high barriers to entry, low rivalry, and strong pricing power. Unattractive industries are commodity-like.
Red flags: frequent acquisitions, aggressive accounting, high insider selling, excessive stock-based compensation, consistently missing guidance, declining margins with no explanation.

Free cash flow is the ultimate measure of financial health. FCF = operating cash flow - capex. Companies consistently generating strong FCF have flexibility to invest, pay dividends, or buy back shares.

Competitive analysis: use Porter Five Forces to assess industry attractiveness. Threat of new entrants, supplier power, buyer power, threat of substitutes, competitive rivalry. Each affects pricing power and profitability.

Earnings quality: high-quality earnings come from core business operations not one-time items, accounting changes, or asset sales. Read footnotes to identify non-recurring items hiding in the numbers.
Avoid value traps: cheap stocks without moats stay cheap or get cheaper. A low P/E without a competitive advantage is a signal of poor quality not a bargain. Cheapness alone is insufficient.

Growth vs value: growth stocks require higher growth rates to justify premiums. Value stocks need stable earnings to realize re-rating. Neither is inherently superior. Moat + margin of safety transcends both styles.
ROE decomposition (DuPont): ROE = Net Margin x Asset Turnover x Financial Leverage. High ROE from margins and turnover is good. High ROE from leverage is dangerous. Decompose to find the source.
Margin of safety sizing: buy 10% below fair value (narrow), 20-30% below (wide), 50%+ below (extreme). The wider the margin the more room for error. Wider margins in uncertain environments.

Three cryptoasset categories: (1) Currencies (Bitcoin Litecoin) store of value and medium of exchange (2) Platforms (Ethereum) for building decentralized applications (3) Utility tokens for specific network services.

Bitcoin valuation framework: Metcalfe Law states network value proportional to square of active users. As Bitcoin adoption grows its value increases exponentially not linearly. Track active addresses as key metric.

Portfolio allocation: suggested 1-5% crypto allocation for conservative investors 5-10% for moderate and up to 20% for aggressive. Small allocation captures upside while limiting catastrophic downside risk.

Cryptoassets are the second generation of the Internet. Blockchain technology enables decentralized trust and value transfer without intermediaries. This is a once-in-a-generation infrastructure shift.

Bitcoin is digital gold: fixed supply cap of 21 million coins, predictable issuance schedule, decentralized network, censorship-resistant. Store of value thesis strengthened by halving events reducing supply inflation.
Ethereum is a world computer: programmable blockchain enabling smart contracts and decentralized applications. Value driven by network usage (gas fees) and total value locked in DeFi protocols.

Diversify within crypto: do not put everything in Bitcoin. Spread across currencies, platforms, and utility tokens. Correlation between crypto assets is high during crashes but diverges in bull markets.

Token velocity: tokens used only for transactions (high velocity) are worth less than tokens with staking or holding incentives (low velocity). Look for mechanisms that reduce token velocity to support price.
Network value to transactions ratio (NVT): crypto equivalent of P/E ratio. NVT = Market Cap / Daily Transaction Volume. High NVT suggests overvaluation. Low NVT suggests undervaluation.

Crypto portfolio rebalancing: rebalance quarterly or when allocations drift more than 5% from targets. Crypto volatility causes rapid allocation drift. Rebalancing systematically sells high and buys low.
Halving cycle: Bitcoin halving every ~4 years reduces block reward by 50%. Historically followed by major bull runs 12-18 months post-halving as reduced supply meets growing demand.

Blockchain technology adoption follows Internet adoption patterns: slow early growth then explosive exponential uptake. We are still early in blockchain adoption curve comparable to Internet in mid-1990s.
Security: use hardware wallets for long-term storage. Never leave significant holdings on exchanges. Self-custody is the fundamental principle of crypto ownership. Not your keys not your coins.

Total addressable market approach: estimate the size of the market a cryptoasset could disrupt. Bitcoin could capture portion of gold market ($12T). Ethereum could capture portion of financial services ($26T).
Utility token valuation: value = (size of market addressed) x (token share of transactions) / (token velocity). This network utility model provides a framework for fundamental crypto valuation.

Regulatory risk management: diversify geographically, avoid tokens likely to be classified as securities, stay informed on regulatory developments. Regulation can be existential for individual projects.
Bitcoin dominance cycle: when Bitcoin dominance rises altcoins underperform. When Bitcoin dominance falls altcoins outperform. Track BTC.D as a regime indicator for crypto allocation strategy.
White paper analysis: evaluate team credentials, problem being solved, technical approach, token economics, roadmap credibility, and community engagement before investing in any cryptoasset project.
Dollar-cost average into crypto positions over 3-6 months. Crypto volatility makes lump-sum entry psychologically difficult and timing-dependent. DCA reduces entry timing risk substantially.

On-chain metrics: track active addresses, hash rate, transaction count, exchange inflows/outflows. These provide fundamental demand signals not available for traditional assets. On-chain analysis is crypto-specific edge.
Altcoin survival rate is low: historically 90%+ of altcoins lose most of their value over 3-5 years. Stick to top 20-30 by market cap unless you have deep technical understanding of smaller projects.
Lindy effect for Bitcoin: the longer Bitcoin survives the longer it is expected to survive. Each year of operation increases confidence in its longevity and store-of-value properties.
Never invest more in crypto than you can afford to lose completely. Crypto is a high-risk high-reward asset class. Assume any individual position could go to zero and size accordingly.
The 2% rule: never risk more than 2% of total capital on a single trade. This preserves capital during losing streaks and allows 50+ consecutive losses before ruin. Professional standard.
Position sizing formula: Position Size = (Account x Risk%) / (Entry - Stop). This mathematical approach ensures consistent risk regardless of asset price or volatility. Apply before every trade.
Risk-reward minimum 1:2. Risk $1 to make $2. With 1:3 R:R you only need 33% win rate to be profitable. Calculate before entering. If R:R does not meet minimum skip the trade.
Trading success is 80% psychology 20% methodology. The best strategy fails if you cannot execute consistently. Master your emotions before optimizing your system. Mindset is the edge.

Five fundamental truths: (1) Anything can happen (2) You do not need to know what happens next to make money (3) Wins and losses are randomly distributed (4) An edge is just higher probability (5) Every moment is unique.
Loss recovery protocol: after 3 consecutive losses reduce size by 50%, after 10% drawdown stop trading for 48 hours, after 20% drawdown switch to paper trading until system confidence restored.

Keep a detailed trading journal. Record every trade with entry reason, exit reason, emotions at entry, emotions at exit, and post-trade review. Patterns emerge that reveal psychological edges and weaknesses.
Never average down. If a position moves against you your original thesis is being challenged. Adding to losers doubles risk on a losing idea. Only add to winners who have proven the thesis correct.
Correlation management: ensure portfolio positions are not all exposed to the same risk factor. During crises correlations spike to 1.0. Diversify across factors not just tickers.
Process over outcomes. A good trade can lose money and a bad trade can make money. Judge yourself on the quality of your decision-making process not on individual trade outcomes.

Multi-timeframe analysis: use higher timeframe for trend direction, middle timeframe for setup identification, lower timeframe for precision entry. All three must align for highest probability trades.
Maximum portfolio risk: total portfolio heat (sum of all open position risks) should not exceed 6-8%. If all positions hit stops simultaneously the drawdown is manageable and recoverable.
Revenge trading is the account killer. After a loss the urge to make it back immediately is overwhelming. Resist it completely. Take a break, review the loss, and wait for the next valid setup.
Trailing stops: use ATR-based or percentage-based trailing stops to lock in profits on winning positions. Let winners run but protect gains. Trail by 2-3x ATR for trend-following positions.

Use limit orders instead of market orders when possible. Factor in slippage when calculating position size and risk-reward. In low-liquidity markets slippage can significantly erode theoretical returns.
The 80/20 rule: 80% of profits come from 20% of trades. Identify your best setup types and trade them exclusively. Eliminate low-probability setups even if they occasionally work.

Kelly Criterion for position sizing: Kelly% = W - ((1-W)/R) where W=win rate R=avg win/avg loss. Use half-Kelly for conservative sizing. Full Kelly is mathematically optimal but psychologically brutal.
Backtest any strategy over at least 10 years of data across different market regimes (trending ranging volatile calm). Out-of-sample testing is critical. In-sample optimization is curve-fitting.

Identify market regime before applying strategy. Trend-following works in trending markets. Mean-reversion works in ranging markets. Applying wrong strategy to wrong regime is the most common systematic error.
Amateurs think about how much they can make. Professionals think about how much they can lose. Shift your mindset from upside fantasy to downside protection. Survival comes before profits.
Scaling out: exit 50% at 1:2 R:R move stop to breakeven on remainder let 25% run to 1:3 R:R and trail final 25% with trailing stop. Captures profit while maintaining upside optionality.
Fear of missing out (FOMO) leads to chasing trades at bad prices. Missing one trade costs nothing. Chasing it can cost everything. The market will offer another opportunity. Patience is free alpha.

Interest rate environment affects all asset classes. Rising rates favor value over growth, short duration over long duration, and financials over technology. Understand the macro regime before sector allocation.

Hedging: use options, inverse ETFs, or negatively correlated positions to reduce portfolio risk during uncertain periods. The cost of hedging is insurance. Small premium protects against catastrophic loss.
Separate self-worth from trading performance. A losing trade does not make you a bad person. A winning trade does not make you a genius. Evaluate yourself on process quality not outcomes.
Best trading hours: first and last hour of market session for stocks, London-NY overlap for forex. Liquidity and volatility are highest during these windows. Avoid trading during lunch lull.
The law of survival: (1) Preserve capital (2) Consistent returns (3) Exceptional returns. Cannot achieve #3 without #1 and #2. Survival is the first and most important objective of every trader.
Develop complete confidence in your ability to take losses. If you cannot accept a small loss you will eventually take a catastrophic one. Small losses are the cost of doing business in markets.
Walk-forward optimization: optimize on historical data then test on out-of-sample period. Repeat rolling forward. This prevents overfitting and ensures strategy works in unseen market conditions.
Volume and volatility regime: high volume + high volatility = trending market (trade breakouts). Low volume + low volatility = range-bound (trade mean reversion). Match strategy to regime.

Sector rotation: money flows from defensive sectors (utilities consumer staples) to growth sectors (technology consumer discretionary) as economic cycle progresses. Track sector relative performance for cycle position.
Automated trade management: set stop loss and take profit simultaneously with entry. Use OCO orders. Eliminates emotional decisions during the trade. Automation enforces discipline.

The law of diminishing returns: more indicators do not improve results. More screen time does not equal more profits. Overtrading kills accounts. Simpler systems are more robust and easier to execute.

Correlation breakdown: during black swan events all correlations go to 1.0. Your diversification fails when you need it most. Have a plan for total portfolio correlation spike. Cash is the only true hedge.

Liquidity drives all markets short-term. When central bank liquidity expands asset prices rise. When liquidity contracts asset prices fall. Track M2 money supply and Fed balance sheet for liquidity regime.
Do not confuse a bull market with genius. In rising markets everyone looks smart. The true test of skill comes in bear markets and volatile periods. Stay humble during winning streaks.

Maximum drawdown tolerance: define your maximum acceptable drawdown before starting. If hit, stop trading and reassess. A 50% loss requires 100% gain. A 20% loss requires 25%. Prevention is easier than recovery.
Credit spreads signal risk appetite. Widening credit spreads (junk bond yields rising vs treasuries) indicate rising risk aversion and potential equity weakness. Narrowing spreads signal risk-on.
Pre-trade checklist: (1) Trend confirmed (2) At key level (3) Pattern confirmed (4) Volume OK (5) R:R meets minimum (6) Stop in place (7) Size calculated (8) No major news pending. All must pass.
Tail risk hedging: allocate 1-3% of portfolio annually to far-out-of-the-money puts or similar instruments that pay off exponentially during black swan events. Small cost for catastrophic protection.
Develop routines and rituals to maintain consistency. Same preparation time, same analysis process, same execution approach. Routine eliminates decision fatigue and reduces emotional variability.

Funding rate extremes in crypto derivatives signal crowded positioning. Extreme positive funding = overleveraged longs vulnerable to liquidation cascade. Extreme negative = overleveraged shorts. Both signal reversals.
MVRV Z-Score above 7 signals Bitcoin overvaluation historically. Below 0 signals undervaluation. Use as a regime filter for crypto allocation not as a precise timing tool.
SOPR (Spent Output Profit Ratio) reset to 1.0 after a correction indicates profit-taking has exhausted. When SOPR resets from above to 1.0 and bounces it often signals continuation of uptrend.
Open Interest divergence: if price makes new high but OI declines the move lacks conviction and may reverse. Price + rising OI = strong trend. Price + falling OI = weak trend prone to reversal.

Delta divergence: if price rises but delta (buy-sell volume) turns negative smart money is distributing into strength. This divergence often precedes reversals. Track delta for institutional footprint.
Wyckoff spring: price briefly dips below support on low volume then quickly reverses upward. This shakeout traps shorts and signals accumulation by smart money. High-probability long entry signal.

Liquidation cascade awareness: when a large number of leveraged positions face liquidation, price can move violently and quickly. Identify liquidation levels using OI data and funding rates to avoid being caught.

Bitcoin halving proximity: historically BTC rallies 12-18 months after halving as reduced supply issuance meets growing demand. Halving years tend to see accumulation. Post-halving sees parabolic advance.

Alt-season detection: when Bitcoin dominance falls while total crypto market cap rises, capital rotates into altcoins. Alt-season typically follows Bitcoin consolidation after major moves. Track BTC.D + TOTAL chart.

FOMC dates create volatility events. Reduce position sizes or hedge before major Fed announcements. The 48 hours around FOMC decisions have statistically higher volatility and larger directional moves.

Liquidity expansion phases: when central banks inject liquidity (QE, rate cuts) asset prices inflate across all classes. Position long in risk assets. Liquidity contraction (QT, rate hikes) favors defensive positioning.

Volume profile analysis: identify high-volume nodes (fair value) and low-volume nodes (rejection areas). Price tends to return to high-volume nodes and move quickly through low-volume nodes. Use for entry/exit levels.

Overfitting is the cardinal sin of backtesting. If your strategy has too many parameters it fits noise not signal. Fewer parameters = more robust. A simple strategy that works on multiple markets is better than a complex one on one market.
Accept uncertainty. The market is not a puzzle to be solved but a probability distribution to be navigated. No amount of analysis gives certainty. The best traders are comfortable with not knowing.
